Honey is a sweet, viscous liquid produced by bees, and while it can be a nutritious and healthy addition to a child's diet after the age of 12 months, it can pose a serious health risk to infants under that age.

The American Academy of Pediatrics and other reputable health organizations advise against giving honey to infants under 12 months of age due to the risk of botulism. Botulism is a potentially life-threatening bacterial infection that can cause muscle paralysis, respiratory failure, and even death. Infants under 12 months of age do not have a fully developed digestive system, and their immune system is not yet mature enough to handle the bacteria found in honey.

In addition to the risk of botulism, honey can also cause an allergic reaction in some infants. Honey is a common allergen, and infants who are allergic to honey may experience symptoms such as diarrhea, vomiting, and difficulty breathing.

While it may be tempting to give your infant honey as a treat, it is important to prioritize their health and safety. There are many other safe and healthy alternatives to honey for infants under 12 months of age, such as breast milk or formula, pureed fruits and vegetables, and iron-fortified cereals.

In conclusion, it is not recommended to feed infants under 12 months of age honey due to the risk of botulism and allergic reactions.
